channel.title: Untagged on Fernando Correia's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Untagged on Fernando Correia's Blog

item.title: building a code review bot
item.description:
Building Astra: A PR Review Bot With Deep Context

How I automated the grunt work of code review so that tech leads can focus more on intent and design.

1. The Problem: Thorough code review is necessary but expensive

As a tech lead, reviewing my team’s pull requests is one of the most important parts of my job. A good review asks a lot of questions at once:

  • Is it correct? Does the code do what the PR says it does?
  • Does it solve the problem? Not just the diff in isolation — does it actually address the ticket that spawned it?
  • Is the quality up to par? Readability, naming, idiomatic usage of the language and frameworks.
  • What got missed? Edge cases, security holes, performance cliffs, concurrency bugs.
  • Is it tested? Evidence of automated tests, or at minimum a credible smoke test when end-to-end automation isn’t practical.
  • What’s next? Follow-up work the PR surfaces but shouldn’t absorb — tech debt, adjacent issues, design questions.

Before AI assistants, I did all of this manually: reading the diff, checking out the branch locally, walking the call tree of affected code, cross-referencing library behavior, and drafting comments by hand. It’s thorough, but it’s slow — and for large diffs, it’s exhausting. The reviewer becomes a bottleneck, and the deeper questions (intent, design, scope) get crowded out by the low-level work of spotting every null-check and off-by-one.

item.title: Scratch
item.description:
Arithmetic

We can perform arithmetic in Go with literals (or named values, covered in the next exercise) using the following operators:

  • + to add
  • - to subtract
  • * to multiply
  • / to divide
  • % to take the remainder (the modulus operator) between two numbers.

Go has the following arithmetic operations that perform calculations and update the variable’s value:

+= to add to the variable. -= to subtract from the variable. *= to multiply the variable by a factor. /= to divide the variable by a dividend.
